body:
This ensure correctness of variable used in iteration loop.

Fixes #7775

comment_data.body: Not sure whether we need to test both cases, since they use the same code path...
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -479,6 +479,34 @@ def test_iteration_over_frozendict_with_subscript():
             print(cython.typeof(b + c))
             print(b + c)
 
+
+class A:
+    pass
+
+
+def test_generator_next_node_coercion(N: list[int]):
+    """
+    >>> test_generator_next_node_coercion([A()])  # doctest:+IGNORE_EXCEPTION_DETAIL
+    Traceback (most recent call last):
+    ...
+    TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'A' and 'int'
+    """
+    return any(n < 0 for n in N)
+
+
+def test_iterator_next_node_coercion(N: list[int]):

comment_data.body:
I think the exception detail is a bit confusing here and it might be clearer to omit it and just write `TypeError:`.

The message you show is for pure Python, but Cython checks it earlier and gives `Expected int, got A`.  I was just looking at it and thinking "surely that can't be right and Cython does something different"
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -603,6 +479,34 @@ def test_iteration_over_frozendict_with_subscript():
             print(cython.typeof(b + c))
             print(b + c)
 
+
+class A:
+    pass
+
+
+def test_generator_next_node_coercion(N: list[int]):
+    """
+    >>> test_generator_next_node_coercion([A()])  # doctest:+IGNORE_EXCEPTION_DETAIL
+    Traceback (most recent call last):
+    ...
+    TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'A' and 'int'
+    """
+    return any(n < 0 for n in N)

comment_data.body: It doesn't hurt much to test if it works in both usages. But these are not "PEP-526 variable annotation" tests (e.g. `x: int = 5`) but tests related to for-loop iteration and type inference behaviour. I would drop them into `type_inference.pyx` because they really test how mismatches on a declared item type impact the behaviour of inferred for-loop variables. Without type inference, we'd not have a problem here.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -479,6 +479,34 @@ def test_iteration_over_frozendict_with_subscript():
             print(cython.typeof(b + c))
             print(b + c)
 
+
+class A:
+    pass
+
+
+def test_generator_next_node_coercion(N: list[int]):
+    """
+    >>> test_generator_next_node_coercion([A()])  # doctest:+IGNORE_EXCEPTION_DETAIL
+    Traceback (most recent call last):
+    ...
+    TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'A' and 'int'
+    """
+    return any(n < 0 for n in N)
+
+
+def test_iterator_next_node_coercion(N: list[int]):

comment_data.body: Hmm. You could check `item_type is type_type` here, but I actually wonder why there should be a special case like this. In the concrete failing test, we are iterating over a sequence of exception _instances_. Why should `type_type` as `item_type` be the correct result of the iterator type inference here? Shouldn't it infer a concrete exception type instead?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -3545,10 +3545,11 @@ def infer_type(self, env, iterator_type=None):
 
     def analyse_types(self, env):
         item_type = self.infer_type(env, self.iterator.type)
-        if self.iterator.type.is_pyobject and not item_type.is_pyobject:
-            # We definitely read a Python object from the iterable but inferred a C type for it,
-            # probably by anticipating to unpack it.  Do the coercion outside to allow undoing it later.
-            self.type = item_type.equivalent_type or py_object_type
+        if self.iterator.type.is_pyobject and \
+                not (item_type.is_builtin_type and item_type.name == 'type'):

commit.message:
Check the type of the NextNode.

This ensure correctness of variable used in iteration loop.
Try to fix the assumptions about types read vs. returned.

repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

milestone.description:
Main features:
- Pattern matching
- Generics / subscripted container types
- `@cython.collection_type()`
- `restrict`
- more vectorcalls
- more thread safety
- smaller modules, more shared code
- remove Py3.8 support, keep Py3.9+

title: Check the type of the NextNode.
